children.text: I owe an enormous debt to colleagues who read early drafts of this book at various stages of its long gestation and saved me from more errors than I care to admit. Portions of the argument were first tried out in seminars at the now-defunct Institute for Imaginary Studies, whose participants pushed back hard enough on the earliest, sloppier version of the threshold claim that the book you are holding is a considerably better one for it. I am grateful in particular to the graduate students who sat through two full years of half-formed lectures on this material without once losing patience with my habit of changing my mind in front of them.
children.label: text

children.text: Financial support for the research behind this book came from a small and now entirely defunct foundation grant, administered for two years by a program officer whose patience with delayed reports deserves its own acknowledgement. Earlier versions of chapters two and four were presented at the Seminar on Fictional Method, and I thank the participants there for comments that forced me to abandon an earlier, weaker formulation of the argument. Any errors that remain, needless to say, are mine alone, and not those of the many people who tried, in good faith, to talk me out of them.
children.label: text

children.text: The central claim of this book is that provincial assemblies convene not when discontent is greatest but once discontent crosses what I call the Vexley threshold principle: a point at which previously disconnected grievances abruptly synchronize into a single, shared demand. Below that threshold, grievances remain fragmented and assemblies fail to convene no matter how numerous the individual complaints; above it, convening becomes almost automatic. The chapters that follow trace this threshold across five separate provincial records, showing that the timing of assembly, not its eventual size, is the more diagnostic feature of the underlying mobilization dynamic.
children.label: text
